questions.question: Solve |x − 5| = 12.
questions.explanation: Set up two cases: x − 5 = 12 gives x = 17, and x − 5 = −12 gives x = −7. Both are valid solutions to the absolute value equation.

questions.question: Solve |2x + 3| < 7.
questions.explanation: Rewrite as −7 < 2x + 3 < 7. Subtract 3 from all parts: −10 < 2x < 4. Divide by 2: −5 < x < 2.

questions.question: A temperature sensor has a margin of error of ±3°C. If the actual temperature is 20°C, write and solve an absolute value inequality for the possible measured values.
questions.explanation: The measured value x must be within 3°C of 20°C. Solve |x − 20| ≤ 3 as −3 ≤ x − 20 ≤ 3, giving 17 ≤ x ≤ 23.

questions.question: Solve |3x − 9| = |x + 1|.
questions.explanation: When two absolute values are equal, either 3x − 9 = x + 1 (giving x = 5) or 3x − 9 = −(x + 1) (giving 4x = 8, so x = 1). Verify both in the original equation.

questions.question: For what values of x is |x + 4| ≥ 6?
questions.explanation: Rewrite as x + 4 ≥ 6 or x + 4 ≤ −6. This gives x ≥ 2 or x ≤ −10. The solution is two rays on the number line.

questions.question: Solve |x| + 5 = 13.
questions.explanation: Subtract 5 from both sides: |x| = 8. The absolute value equals 8 when x = 8 or x = −8.
